content:
I've always wondered how programming languages are converted into binary and executed by a computer.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

As I learned more about computer systems, I realized how important bootstrapping has been in the evolution of computing.
Over the years, the way we give instructions to computers has changed dramatically:
• First, programmers used punch cards.
• Then came assembly language, which was closer to the hardware.
• Later, high-level languages such as C made programming much easier and more productive.
At a high level, the process works like this:
1. We write code in a high-level language.
2. A compiler translates that code into lower-level instructions (often assembly language).
3. An assembler converts assembly instructions into machine code (binary).
4. The CPU fetches and decodes these binary instructions.
5. The hardware executes the operations specified by those instructions.
What fascinates me is that each generation of software tools helped create the next generation. This self improving process is often called bootstrapping using existing software and systems to build more advanced software and systems.
What's even more interesting is that modern AI, Machine Learning, and Deep Learning still rely on these same foundations. While the concepts are far more advanced, they ultimately run as programs on computers. Their code is compiled, converted into machine instructions, and executed by hardware just like any other software.
The major difference is the scale of computation. AI systems process enormous amounts of data and perform billions or even trillions of mathematical operations, which is why they require powerful hardware such as GPUs and TPUs.
From punch cards to assembly language, from compilers to AI models, computing has continuously evolved through layers of abstraction. Yet at its core, everything still comes down to the same fundamental idea: giving instructions to hardware in a form it can understand and execute.
